name: Redwood Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Redwood. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal power range for Redwood surface cleaning without charring

wavelength.description: Near-infrared wavelength optimized for Redwood lignin absorption

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning control

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning with thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal without wood damage

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse width for controlled ablation of contaminants

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Recommended number of passes for complete cleaning

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning pattern

dwellTime.description: Optimal dwell time per cleaning spot

power.description: Typical range for wood cleaning at 1064 nm to remove contaminants without charring; starting at 100 W allows controlled ablation. Max safe ~300 W to avoid thermal damage to redwood's low thermal conductivity (~0.1 W/m·K). Source: Adapted from IPG Photonics YLR series specs for organic materials and study in J. Laser Appl. (2018) on wood surface cleaning.

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for contaminant removal; slower for heavy soiling (100-500 mm/s), faster for light (1000-2000 mm/s). Recommended 500 mm/s balances efficiency and heat control on redwood. Source: Industry standards from Laserax equipment manuals and Opt. Lasers Eng. (2020) case studies on wood preparation.

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for 1064 nm cleaning of wood to minimize heat-affected zone (HAZ <50 μm); picosecond (ps) reduces HAZ further but increases cost. Avoid fs for industrial scale. Recommended 20 ns for redwood's reflectivity (~20-30% at 1064 nm). Source: Laser physics research in Appl. Phys. A (2019) on pulsed laser ablation of lignocellulosic materials.

repetition_rate.description: Typical for cleaning applications; higher rates (>50 kHz) speed up process but risk heat accumulation on low-conductivity redwood, leading to carbonization. Recommended 30 kHz for balance. Source: Academic literature in Lasers Med. Sci. (2017) on pulse repetition effects in wood laser processing.

energy_density.description: Fluence range for contaminant removal; redwood damage threshold ~3-5 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to moderate absorption. Recommended 1.5 J/cm² for safe cleaning below threshold. Conservative estimate for variability in wood moisture. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Wood Sci. Technol. (2021) and J. Appl. Phys. (2015).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for cleaning; smaller for fine details (100-200 μm), larger for broad areas (300-500 μm). Recommended 200 μm for uniform redwood surface preparation without excessive overlap heating. Source: Equipment specifications from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaners for industrial wood applications.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy; recommended 2 passes for complete removal on redwood while minimizing exposure time and damage. Source: Industry practice in restoration case studies, e.g., J. Cult. Herit. (2019) on laser cleaning of wooden artifacts.

overlap_ratio.description: Typical for uniform coverage; 30-50% for speed, 50-80% for thoroughness. Recommended 50% to ensure even cleaning on redwood's textured surface without gaps or over-ablation. Source: Process optimization studies in Proc. SPIE (2022) on scan strategies for laser surface treatment.
